skill.name: k8s-manifest-generator
skill.description: Create Kubernetes manifests for Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets, and storage using production-oriented patterns and security guidance.
skill.summary: Generate production-oriented Kubernetes YAML with resource limits, probes, labels, services, config, and secret handling guidance.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, environment, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found no prompt injection or malicious code execution; most high-volume detections are Markdown fences or Kubernetes documentation examples. The remaining risk is medium because the skill can generate deployable YAML containing shell hooks, scripts, Secret placeholders, and public service examples that require human review before use.

medium_findings.title: Deployable Manifests Include Shell Execution Hooks
medium_findings.description: The skill examples include init scripts, healthcheck scripts, and lifecycle exec hooks that run shell commands inside generated Kubernetes resources. This is legitimate Kubernetes functionality, but generated commands can alter startup behavior or mask failures if deployed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are clearly present in manifest examples, but they are Kubernetes configuration snippets rather than hidden marketplace execution code.

medium_findings.title: Secret Examples Contain Plaintext Placeholder Credentials
medium_findings.description: The skill shows Kubernetes Secret examples with placeholder password, API key, certificate, and private key fields. The surrounding guidance warns not to commit real secrets, but users could copy the pattern into repositories without external secret management.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive fields are explicit placeholders and the documentation includes mitigation guidance, so the risk is real but not malicious.

medium_findings.title: Public Service Examples Require Exposure Review
medium_findings.description: The Service templates include LoadBalancer and NodePort patterns, including internet-facing cloud load balancer annotations. These examples are expected for the skill, but publishing generated manifests without source restrictions can expose workloads unintentionally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples intentionally describe public Kubernetes networking patterns. The risk depends on user deployment context and is not evidence of exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fence Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static detections for Ruby or shell backtick execution are Markdown code fences in SKILL.md and reference files. I found no evidence that these fences execute commands during skill use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged characters delimit documentation examples. There is no script runner or dynamic command invocation in these files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded IPs and URLs Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Static network findings point to Kubernetes example values such as 0.0.0.0, documentation CIDR ranges, service DNS names, and localhost health checks. These are not outbound calls made by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values appear in manifest and reference examples. I found no evidence of hidden network requests or credential exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto Static Labels Do Not Match Semantic Context
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections appear to be triggered by ordinary words inside Kubernetes documentation and placeholders. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or insecure crypto implementation in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are headings and prose about Kubernetes resources. No cryptographic operation is present at those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands in Generated Kubernetes Resources
dangerous_patterns.description: Generated or referenced manifests may include /bin/sh lifecycle hooks, netcat wait loops, curl health checks, and migration commands. Users should review commands before applying generated YAML.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell usage is explicit in deployable YAML examples. It is not hidden execution by the skill, but it can affect deployed workloads.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plaintext Secret Placeholder Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation demonstrates Kubernetes Secret stringData fields for passwords, API keys, TLS certificates, and private keys. Real deployments should use external secret management.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The placeholders are visible and paired with safer guidance, so the pattern is risky only if copied with real secret values.

content.value_statement: Teams often miss important Kubernetes settings when writing YAML by hand. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to generate consistent manifests with production defaults.
actual_capabilities: Generates Deployment manifests with replicas, probes, resources, rollout settings, labels, and security contexts.
Creates Service examples for ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, headless, and multi-port workloads.
Provides ConfigMap patterns for key-value settings, embedded config files, scripts, and Prometheus configuration.
Guides Secret usage with warnings for external secret managers, rotation, RBAC, and TLS secret types.
Suggests production checks for image tags, resource limits, pod scheduling, storage, and manifest validation.
limitations: It does not connect to a Kubernetes cluster or verify live cluster compatibility.
Generated YAML still needs review against your organization security policies.
Secret examples are placeholders and must not be used as real credentials.
Cloud provider annotations may need updates for your Kubernetes version and provider.

use_cases.title: Create a New Service Release
use_cases.description: Generate a Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, and Secret outline for a new containerized application.

use_cases.title: Standardize Platform Templates
use_cases.description: Use consistent labels, probes, resources, security contexts, and service patterns across team manifests.

use_cases.title: Review Production Readiness
use_cases.description: Compare planned manifests against common production checks before committing them to GitOps workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Web App Manifest
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple stateless service
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate Kubernetes manifests for a web app named orders-api using image registry.example.com/orders-api:1.2.3 on port 8080.

prompt_templates.title: Production Deployment Set
prompt_templates.scenario: Add resource, health, and security defaults
prompt_templates.prompt: Create production-ready Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, and Secret manifests for a backend API with three replicas, probes, and non-root security settings.

prompt_templates.title: External Service Exposure
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose a safe service exposure model
prompt_templates.prompt: Design Service manifests for an internal API and a public frontend. Include notes about source range restrictions and cloud load balancer annotations.

prompt_templates.title: GitOps-Ready Manifest Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Kubernetes manifests for production readiness. Check labels, probes, resources, secret handling, rollout behavior, and security context settings.

output_examples.input: A Node.js API needs Kubernetes manifests with health checks and configuration.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a Deployment and Service plan with probe paths, resource limits, environment configuration, labels, and security context recommendations.

output_examples.input: A team needs a public frontend and private backend service layout.
output_examples.output: The skill describes appropriate Service types, exposure boundaries, port mappings, source range considerations, and provider annotation review points.

output_examples.input: A platform team wants reusable ConfigMap and Secret guidance.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines non-sensitive configuration patterns, secret manager recommendations, rotation practices, and warnings against committing real secrets.

best_practices: Validate generated manifests with server-side dry runs and policy tools before deployment.
Replace all placeholder values and review generated shell hooks before applying manifests.
Use external secret management for real credentials instead of committing Secret data.
anti_patterns: Do not deploy generated examples without replacing placeholder images, ports, and credentials.
Do not expose LoadBalancer or NodePort services without network restrictions and approval.
Do not store real passwords, API keys, certificates, or private keys in plain YAML.

faq.question: Does this skill apply manifests to a cluster?
faq.answer: No. It generates and reviews manifest content, but it does not run kubectl or contact a cluster.

faq.question: Can it generate production-ready Deployments?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for probes, resource limits, rollout strategy, labels, and security contexts.

faq.question: Does it handle Kubernetes Secrets safely?
faq.answer: It provides placeholder Secret examples and recommends external secret management for real credentials.

faq.question: Can it create public Services?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes LoadBalancer and NodePort examples, but users must review exposure and source restrictions.

faq.question: Is it tied to one cloud provider?
faq.answer: No. It includes common Kubernetes patterns and examples for provider-specific Service annotations.

faq.question: What should users check before deployment?
faq.answer: Users should check placeholders, policies, namespaces, RBAC, image tags, secrets, and network exposure.
